ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a turmeric-marinated chicken burger with sambal and slaw. FOODON_00002383 (chicken sandwich food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FAN (Chicken burger) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for turmeric-marinated sambal chicken burger variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific fusion chicken burger preparation.

serving.description: 1 burger (with bun, chicken patty, sambal, mayo, slaw)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately one assembled chicken burger weighing around 250 g, comprising a turmeric-marinated chicken fillet, sambal, mayonnaise, coleslaw, and a ciabatta bap or brioche bun.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken burger, fast food type — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; adapted with sambal and slaw component estimates from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for turmeric-marinated sambal chicken burger exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component-level aggregation: grilled/fried chicken fillet (~40% of serving weight, USDA FDC #171477 chicken thigh, cooked), brioche/ciabatta bun (~30%, USDA FDC #325871 hamburger bun), mayonnaise (~8%, USDA FDC #172741), sambal chilli paste (~7%, USDA FDC #170918 chilli sauce proxy), coleslaw (~15%, USDA FDC #169977). Per-100g values are derived from weighted blend of these components normalised to 100 g. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Hamburger bun / mixed chicken sandwich GI — analogous estimate based on white bread bun GI and mixed-meal attenuation from protein and fat components; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific turmeric chicken burger. GI estimated at 54 using the mixed-meal GI calculation approach: the bun component (white bread GI ~70, brioche ~65) is attenuated by the high protein (chicken) and fat (mayo, slaw dressing) content of the overall burger, consistent with published mixed-meal GI reduction methodology (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 56.25) / 100 ≈ 30.4 g (carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 22.5 × 2.5 = 56.25 g). GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per serving) from sambal, mayonnaise, and seasoned chicken
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this burger due to its high sodium load (~1200 mg per serving, approximately 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-sauce options or consume only half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~30 g per serving) driven by refined bun carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the refined bun (ciabatta or brioche) contributes a moderate glycemic load of approximately 30 g per serving; choosing a smaller portion, opting for a wholegrain bun if available, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g; ~7.3 g per serving) and high sodium from mayonnaise, sambal, and fried chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit this burger to occasional consumption given its combined saturated fat (~7 g per serving) and high sodium (~1200 mg per serving), and should consider requesting grilled rather than fried chicken and reduced mayonnaise to lower the saturated fat and caloric load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Chick Summer, sold at Riverside Buns (Boat Quay, Singapore) on field survey, is a Singaporean-fusion chicken burger featuring a turmeric-marinated chicken fillet topped with sambal chilli paste, mayonnaise, and coleslaw (slaw), served in either a ciabatta bap or a brioche bun. The turmeric marinade imparts a warm, earthy, mildly spiced flavour to the chicken, while the sambal adds a distinctly Southeast Asian heat and umami depth. The mayonnaise provides creaminess and the slaw contributes a cool, crunchy textural contrast. The brioche bun option yields a slightly sweet, pillowy texture, while the ciabatta bap offers a chewier, crustier bite. Nutritionally, one serving (~250 g) provides approximately 595 kcal, 32 g protein, 28.5 g fat, and 56 g carbohydrate. The dish is relatively high in sodium (~1200 mg per serving) due to the sambal and mayo components, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate (~30 g per serving), driven primarily by the refined bun. The protein content from the chicken fillet supports satiety. This dish reflects the Singaporean hawker-fusion trend of combining Southeast Asian condiments (sambal) with Western burger formats, and is representative of the growing category of spiced chicken burgers in Singapore's casual dining scene. Suitable for non-vegetarian, non-vegan diners; halal status should be confirmed with the restaurant.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific turmeric-marinated sambal chicken burger. Values are estimated by component-level aggregation from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries (chicken thigh cooked, hamburger bun, mayonnaise, chilli sauce, coleslaw). GI is an analogous estimate using published mixed-meal GI methodology. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no exact record for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any other primary approved source. Estimation methodology documented in nutrient_source.source_match_notes. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation. 3. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on mixed-meal GI attenuation methodology (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008); gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 4. Halal status is not confirmed; the restaurant should be consulted directly. 5. The food_name 'Hot Chick Summer' is a promotional menu name; the canonical English name used in this record is 'Turmeric Chicken Burger with Sambal and Slaw'. 6. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate for a standard assembled chicken burger of this type. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component-level USDA FDC data and carry medium uncertainty. 8. Cuisine is classified as Singaporean-fusion reflecting the Southeast Asian sambal component within a Western burger format, served by a Singapore restaurant.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with component-level nutrient estimation from USDA FDC. Identified that promotional food name 'Hot Chick Summer' needed to be replaced with canonical English name.

Iteration 2: Verified GI category threshold (GI 54 ≤ 55 = low, correct). Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list. Verified vitamin_d_mcg -1 sentinel documented in notes. Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria. Confirmed PASS at score 8/8.
